It is probably best for the definition of a Moore space to include the condition that $M(G, n)$ be simply-connected if $n > 1$. The spaces we construct will have this property.

deps.title: Isomorphism of cellular and singular homology
deps.label: thm:cellular-singular-homology-isomorphic

entries.label: ex:cellular-homology-cpn
entries.title: Cellular homology of CPn
entries.body: By taking a wedge sum of the Moore spaces constructed in the preceding example for varying $n$ we obtain a connected CW complex with any prescribed sequence of homology groups in dimensions $1, 2, 3, \cdots$.

deps.title: CW structure on complex projective space $\CP^n$
deps.label: ex:complex-projective-space-cw

deps.title: Isomorphism of cellular and singular homology
deps.label: thm:cellular-singular-homology-isomorphic

entries.label: ex:cellular-homology-rpn
entries.title: Cellular homology of $\mathbb{RP}^n$
entries.body: As we saw in Example 0.4, $\mathbb{RP}^n$ has a CW structure with one cell $e^k$ in each dimension $k \le n$, and the attaching map for $e^k$ is the 2-sheeted covering projection $\varphi: S^{k-1} \to \mathbb{RP}^{k-1}$. To compute the boundary map $d_k$, we compute the degree of the composition $S^{k-1} \xrightarrow{\varphi} \mathbb{RP}^{k-1} \xrightarrow{q} \mathbb{RP}^{k-1}/\mathbb{RP}^{k-2} = S^{k-1}$, with $q$ the quotient map. The map $q\varphi$ restricts to a homeomorphism from each component of $S^{k-1} - S^{k-2}$ onto $\mathbb{RP}^{k-1} - \mathbb{RP}^{k-2}$, and these two homeomorphisms are obtained from each other by precomposing with the antipodal map of $S^{k-1}$, which has degree $(-1)^k$. Hence $\deg q\varphi = \deg \mathbb{1} + \deg(-\mathbb{1}) = 1 + (-1)^k$, and so $d_k$ is either 0 or multiplication by 2 according to whether $k$ is odd or even. Thus the cellular chain complex for $\mathbb{RP}^n$ is

deps.title: CW structure on real projective space $\RP^n$
deps.label: ex:real-projective-space-cw

deps.title: Isomorphism of cellular and singular homology
deps.label: thm:cellular-singular-homology-isomorphic

entries.label: ex:lens-spaces
entries.title: Lens Spaces
entries.body: This example is somewhat more complicated. Given an integer $m > 1$ and integers $\ell_1, \ldots, \ell_n$ relatively prime to $m$, define the \textbf{lens space} $L = L_m(\ell_1, \ldots, \ell_n)$ to be the orbit space $S^{2n-1}/\mathbb{Z}_m$ of the unit sphere $S^{2n-1} \subset \mathbb{C}^n$ with the action of $\mathbb{Z}_m$ generated by the rotation $\rho(z_1, \ldots, z_n) = (e^{2\pi i\ell_1/m} z_1, \ldots, e^{2\pi i\ell_n/m} z_n)$, rotating the $j^{\text{th}}$ $\mathbb{C}$ factor of $\mathbb{C}^n$ by the angle $2\pi \ell_j/m$. In particular, when $m = 2$, $\rho$ is the antipodal map, so $L = \mathbb{RP}^{2n-1}$ in this case. In the general case, the projection $S^{2n-1} \to L$ is a covering space since the action of $\mathbb{Z}_m$ on $S^{2n-1}$ is free; only the identity element fixes any point of $S^{2n-1}$ since each point of $S^{2n-1}$ has some coordinate $z_j$ nonzero and then $e^{2\pi ik\ell_j/m} z_j \ne z_j$ for $0 < k < m$, as a result of the assumption that $\ell_j$ is relatively prime to $m$.

deps.title: Isomorphism of cellular and singular homology
deps.label: thm:cellular-singular-homology-isomorphic

entries.label: thm:euler-characteristic-homology
entries.title: Euler characteristic in terms of homology
entries.body: $\chi(X) = \sum_n (-1)^n \text{rank} H_n(X)$.

entries.label: lem:splitting-lemma
entries.title: Splitting Lemma
entries.body:
For a short exact sequence $0 \to A \xrightarrow{i} B \xrightarrow{j} C \to 0$ of abelian groups the following statements are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] There is a homomorphism $p: B \to A$ such that $pi = \mathbb{1}: A \to A$.
\item[(b)] There is a homomorphism $s: C \to B$ such that $js = \mathbb{1}: C \to C$.
\item[(c)] There is an isomorphism $B \cong A \oplus C$ making a commutative diagram as at the right, where
\[
\begin{array}{ccccccccc}
0 & \to & A & \xrightarrow{i} & B & \xrightarrow{j} & C & \to & 0 \\
  &     &   &                & \cong &               &   &     &  \\
0 & \to & A & \xrightarrow{a \mapsto (a,0)} & A \oplus C & \xrightarrow{(a,c) \mapsto c} & C & \to & 0
\end{array}
\]
the maps in the lower row are the obvious ones, $a \mapsto (a, 0)$ and $(a, c) \mapsto c$.
\end{enumerate}

If these conditions are satisfied, the exact sequence is said to \textbf{split}. Note that (c) is symmetric: There is no essential difference between the roles of $A$ and $C$.

entries.label: prop:fundamental-group-kg1-torsionfree
entries.title: Fundamental group of finite-dimensional K(G,1) is torsionfree
entries.body: If a finite-dimensional CW complex $X$ is a $K(G,1)$, then the group $G = \pi_1(X)$ must be torsionfree.

entries.label: ex:mayer-vietoris-sphere
entries.title: Mayer-Vietoris sequence of sphere split into hemispheres
entries.body: Take $X = S^n$ with $A$ and $B$ the northern and southern hemispheres, so that $A \cap B = S^{n-1}$. Then in the reduced Mayer-Vietoris sequence the terms $\tilde{H}_i(A)$ and $\tilde{H}_i(B)$ are zero, so we obtain isomorphisms $\tilde{H}_i(S^n) \cong \tilde{H}_{i-1}(S^{n-1})$. This gives another way of calculating the homology groups of $S^n$ by induction.

deps.title: Given subspaces $Z \subset A \subset X$ such that the closure of $Z$ is contained in the interior of $A$, then the inclusion $(X-Z,A-Z) \hookrightarrow (X,A)$ induces isomorphisms $H_n(X-Z,A-Z)\to H_n(X,A)$ for all $n$. Equivalently, for subspaces $A, B \subset X$ whose interiors cover $X$, the inclusion $(B,A \cap B) \hookrightarrow (X,A)$ induces isomorphisms $H_n(B,A\cap B)\to H_n(X,A)$ for all $n$
deps.label: thm:excision-general

entries.label: ex:mayer-vietoris-klein-bottle
entries.title: Mayer-Vietoris sequence of Klein bottle
entries.body: We can decompose the Klein bottle $K$ as the union of two Möbius bands $A$ and $B$ glued together by a homeomorphism between their boundary circles.

deps.title: Given subspaces $Z \subset A \subset X$ such that the closure of $Z$ is contained in the interior of $A$, then the inclusion $(X-Z,A-Z) \hookrightarrow (X,A)$ induces isomorphisms $H_n(X-Z,A-Z)\to H_n(X,A)$ for all $n$. Equivalently, for subspaces $A, B \subset X$ whose interiors cover $X$, the inclusion $(B,A \cap B) \hookrightarrow (X,A)$ induces isomorphisms $H_n(B,A\cap B)\to H_n(X,A)$ for all $n$
deps.label: thm:excision-general

entries.label: ex:homology-mapping-torus
entries.title: Homology of mapping torus
entries.body: Let us describe an exact sequence which is somewhat similar to the Mayer-Vietoris sequence and which in some cases generalizes it. If we are given two maps $f, g: X \to Y$ then we can form a quotient space $Z$ of the disjoint union of $X \times I$ and $Y$ via the identifications $(x, 0) \sim f(x)$ and $(x, 1) \sim g(x)$, thus attaching one end of $X \times I$ to $Y$ by $f$ and the other end by $g$. For example, if $f$ and $g$ are each the identity map $X \to X$ then $Z = X \times S^1$. If only one of $f$ and $g$, say $f$, is the identity map, then $Z$ is homeomorphic to what is called the mapping torus of $g$, the quotient space of $X \times I$ under the identifications $(x, 0) \sim (g(x), 1)$. The Klein bottle is an example, with $g$ a reflection $S^1 \to S^1$.

deps.title: Given subspaces $Z \subset A \subset X$ such that the closure of $Z$ is contained in the interior of $A$, then the inclusion $(X-Z,A-Z) \hookrightarrow (X,A)$ induces isomorphisms $H_n(X-Z,A-Z)\to H_n(X,A)$ for all $n$. Equivalently, for subspaces $A, B \subset X$ whose interiors cover $X$, the inclusion $(B,A \cap B) \hookrightarrow (X,A)$ induces isomorphisms $H_n(B,A\cap B)\to H_n(X,A)$ for all $n$
deps.label: thm:excision-general

entries.label: lem:homology-map-degree-m-coefficients
entries.title: Homology map of degree m map with G coefficients
entries.body: If $f: S^k \to S^k$ has degree $m$, then $f_*: H_k(S^k; G) \to H_k(S^k; G)$ is multiplication by $m$.

entries.label: ex:homology-rpn-field-coefficients
entries.title: Homology of RPn with field coefficients
entries.body: It is instructive to see what happens to the homology of $\mathbb{R}P^n$ when the coefficient group $G$ is chosen to be a field $F$. The cellular chain complex is

entries.label: ex:homology-moore-space-quotient
entries.title: Homology of Moore space quotient
entries.body:
Let $X$ be a Moore space $M(\mathbb{Z}_m, n)$ obtained from $S^n$ by attaching a cell $e^{n+1}$ by a map of degree $m$. The quotient map $f:X \to X/S^n = S^{n+1}$ induces trivial homomorphisms on reduced homology with $\mathbb{Z}$ coefficients since the nonzero reduced homology groups of $X$ and $S^{n+1}$ occur in different dimensions. But with $\mathbb{Z}_m$ coefficients the story is different, as we can see by considering the long exact sequence of the pair $(X, S^n)$, which contains the segment
\[
0 = \widetilde{H}_{n+1}(S^n;\mathbb{Z}_m) \to \widetilde{H}_{n+1}(X;\mathbb{Z}_m) \xrightarrow{f_*} \widetilde{H}_{n+1}(X/S^n;\mathbb{Z}_m)
\]
Exactness says that $f_*$ is injective, hence nonzero since $\widetilde{H}_{n+1}(X;\mathbb{Z}_m)$ is $\mathbb{Z}_m$, the cellular boundary map $H_{n+1}(X^{n+1}, X^n;\mathbb{Z}_m) \to H_n(X^n, X^{n-1};\mathbb{Z}_m)$ being $\mathbb{Z}_m \xrightarrow{m} \mathbb{Z}_m$.

entries.label: thm:hurewicz-dimension-1
entries.title: Hurewicz theorem in dimension 1
entries.body: By regarding loops as singular 1-cycles, we obtain a homomorphism $h: \pi_1(X, x_0) \to H_1(X)$. If $X$ is path-connected, then $h$ is surjective and has kernel the commutator subgroup of $\pi_1(X)$, so $h$ induces an isomorphism from the abelianization of $\pi_1(X)$ onto $H_1(X)$.

entries.label: ex:h1-closed-orientable-surface
entries.title: H1 of closed orientable surface
entries.body: For the closed orientable surface $M$ of genus $g$, the abelianization of $\pi_1(M)$ is $\mathbb{Z}^{2g}$, the product of $2g$ copies of $\mathbb{Z}$, and a basis for $H_1(M)$ consists of the 1-cycles represented by the 1-cells of $M$ in its standard CW structure. We can also represent a basis by the loops $\alpha_i$ and $\beta_i$ shown in the figure below since these

entries.label: prop:homology-sphere-complement-disk-sphere
entries.title: Homology of sphere complement of disk/sphere
entries.body:
(a) For an embedding $h:D^k \to S^n$, $\tilde{H}_i(S^n - h(D^k)) = 0$ for all $i$.
(b) For an embedding $h:S^k \to S^n$ with $k < n$, $\tilde{H}_i(S^n - h(S^k))$ is $\mathbb{Z}$ for $i = n - k - 1$ and $0$ otherwise.

deps.title: Mayer-Vietoris sequence of sphere split into hemispheres
deps.label: ex:mayer-vietoris-sphere

deps.title: Given subspaces $Z \subset A \subset X$ such that the closure of $Z$ is contained in the interior of $A$, then the inclusion $(X-Z,A-Z) \hookrightarrow (X,A)$ induces isomorphisms $H_n(X-Z,A-Z)\to H_n(X,A)$ for all $n$. Equivalently, for subspaces $A, B \subset X$ whose interiors cover $X$, the inclusion $(B,A \cap B) \hookrightarrow (X,A)$ induces isomorphisms $H_n(B,A\cap B)\to H_n(X,A)$ for all $n$
deps.label: thm:excision-general

entries.label: ex:alexander-horned-sphere
entries.title: The Alexander Horned Sphere
entries.body:
This is a subspace $S \subset \mathbb{R}^3$ homeomorphic to $S^2$ such that the unbounded component of $\mathbb{R}^3 - S$ is not simply-connected
as it is for the standard $S^2 \subset \mathbb{R}^3$. We will construct $S$ by defining a sequence of compact subspaces $X_0 \supset X_1 \supset \cdots$ of $\mathbb{R}^3$ whose intersection is homeomorphic to a ball,
and then $S$ will be the boundary sphere of this ball.

deps.title: Homology of sphere complement of disk/sphere
deps.label: prop:homology-sphere-complement-disk-sphere

entries.label: thm:invariance-of-domain-general
entries.title: Invariance of domain
entries.body:
If $U$ is an open set in $\mathbb{R}^n$ and $h:U \to \mathbb{R}^n$ is an embedding, or more
generally just a continuous injection, then the image $h(U)$ is an open set in $\mathbb{R}^n$.

deps.title: Homology of sphere complement of disk/sphere
deps.label: prop:homology-sphere-complement-disk-sphere

entries.label: cor:embedding-compact-manifold-connected
entries.title: Embedding of compact manifold in connected manifold
entries.body:
If $M$ is a compact $n$-manifold and $N$ is a connected $n$-manifold,
then an embedding $h:M \to N$ must be surjective, hence a homeomorphism.

entries.label: thm:real-complex-division-algebras
entries.title: Real and complex division algebras
entries.body: $\mathbb{R}$ and $\mathbb{C}$ are the only finite-dimensional division algebras over $\mathbb{R}$ which are commutative and have an identity.

deps.title: Only Z2 acts freely on even spheres
deps.label: prop:z2-acts-freely-even-spheres

entries.label: prop:odd-map-odd-degree
entries.title: Odd map of Sn has odd degree
entries.body: An odd map $f : S^n \to S^n$, satisfying $f(-x) = -f(x)$ for all $x$, must have odd degree.

entries.label: cor:borsuk-ulam
entries.title: Borsuk-Ulam theorem
entries.body: For every map $g : S^n \to \mathbb{R}^n$ there exists a point $x \in S^n$ with $g(x) = g(-x)$.

deps.title: Odd map of Sn has odd degree
deps.label: prop:odd-map-odd-degree

entries.label: thm:simplicial-approximation
entries.title: Simplicial Approximation Theorem
entries.body: If $K$ is a finite simplicial complex and $L$ is an arbitrary simplicial complex, then any map $f : K \to L$ is homotopic to a map that is simplicial with respect to some iterated barycentric subdivision of $K$.

entries.label: lem:star-intersections
entries.title: Intersections of star neighborhoods
entries.body: For vertices $v_1, \ldots, v_n$ of a simplicial complex $X$, the intersection st $v_1 \cap \cdots \cap$ st $v_n$ is empty unless $v_1, \ldots, v_n$ are the vertices of a simplex $\sigma$ of $X$, in which case st $v_1 \cap \cdots \cap$ st $v_n = $ st $\sigma$.

entries.label: thm:lefschetz-fixed-point
entries.title: Lefschetz Fixed Point Theorem
entries.body: If $X$ is a finite simplicial complex, or more generally a retract of a finite simplicial complex, and $f: X \to X$ is a map with $\tau(f) \neq 0$, then $f$ has a fixed point.

deps.title: Isomorphism of cellular and singular homology
deps.label: thm:cellular-singular-homology-isomorphic

entries.label: ex:lefschetz-surface-rotation
entries.title: Lefschetz number of surface rotation
entries.body:
Let us verify the theorem in an example. Let $X$ be the closed orientable surface of genus 3 as shown in the figure below, with $f:X \to X$ the 180
degree rotation about a vertical axis passing through the central hole of
$X$. Since $f$ has no fixed points, we
should have $\tau(f) = 0$. The induced
map $f_* : H_0(X) \to H_0(X)$ is the identity, as always for a path-connected space, so this contributes 1 to $\tau(f)$. For $H_1(X)$
we saw in Example 2A.2 that the six loops $\alpha_i$ and $\beta_i$ represent a basis. The map
$f_*$ interchanges the homology classes of $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_3$, and likewise for $\beta_1$ and $\beta_3$,
while $\beta_2$ is sent to itself and $\alpha_2$ is sent to $\alpha_2'$ which is homologous to $\alpha_2$ as we
saw in Example 2A.2. So $f_* : H_1(X) \to H_1(X)$ contributes $-2$ to $\tau(f)$. It remains
to check that $f_* : H_2(X) \to H_2(X)$ is the identity, which we do by the commutative diagram
at the right, where $x$ is a point of $X$ in the central torus and $y = f(x)$. We can see that the

entries.label: thm:cw-homotopy-equivalent-simplicial
entries.title: CW complex homotopy equivalent to simplicial complex
entries.body:
Every CW complex $X$ is homotopy equivalent to a simplicial complex,
which can be chosen to be of the same dimension as $X$, finite if $X$ is finite, and
countable if $X$ is countable.
